name: CSS Layout & Styling
description: Master CSS layout systems, responsive design, and modern styling techniques that senior devs reach for daily.

cards.front: What is the CSS Box Model?
cards.back:
Every element is a rectangular box with four layers:

1. Content — the actual text/image
2. Padding — space between content and border
3. Border — surrounds the padding
4. Margin — space outside the border

box-sizing: content-box (default) — width/height = content only
box-sizing: border-box — width/height includes padding + border

Best practice: Use border-box globally:
*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; }

cards.front: Explain Flexbox. When do you use it?
cards.back:
Flexbox is a 1-dimensional layout system (row OR column).

Container properties:
• display: flex
• flex-direction: row | column
• justify-content: alignment on main axis
• align-items: alignment on cross axis
• gap: spacing between items

Item properties:
• flex: grow shrink basis (e.g., flex: 1 0 auto)
• align-self: override cross-axis alignment

Use for: navbars, card rows, centering, equal-height columns, any 1D layout.

cards.front: Explain CSS Grid. When do you use it vs Flexbox?
What is CSS specificity and how is it calculated?
cards.back:
Specificity determines which CSS rule wins when multiple rules target the same element.

Specificity hierarchy (highest to lowest):
1. !important (avoid this)
2. Inline styles (style="") — (1,0,0,0)
3. IDs (#header) — (0,1,0,0)
4. Classes, attributes, pseudo-classes (.btn, [type], :hover) — (0,0,1,0)
5. Elements, pseudo-elements (div, ::before) — (0,0,0,1)
6. Universal selector (*) — (0,0,0,0)

Example: #nav .link:hover = (0,1,2,0)
When equal specificity: last rule in source order wins.

cards.front: What is the difference between position values?
cards.back:
• static: default, normal document flow
• relative: offset from normal position, still occupies original space
• absolute: removed from flow, positioned relative to nearest positioned ancestor
• fixed: removed from flow, positioned relative to viewport (stays during scroll)
• sticky: toggles between relative and fixed based on scroll position

sticky example:
.header { position: sticky; top: 0; } — header sticks to top when you scroll past it.

Key: absolute + relative parent is the most common positioning pattern.

cards.front: How do media queries work for responsive design?
What is the stacking context and how does z-index work?
cards.back:
z-index controls layering of positioned elements, but only within the same stacking context.

A new stacking context is created by:
• position + z-index (not auto)
• opacity < 1
• transform, filter, perspective
• isolation: isolate

Common gotcha: z-index: 9999 doesn't work because the element is in a lower stacking context than the element it's trying to cover.

Fix: Use isolation: isolate on containers to create predictable stacking contexts. Avoid z-index wars.

cards.front: What are CSS transitions vs animations?

cards.back:
BEM = Block Element Modifier. A CSS naming methodology:

• Block: standalone component → .card
• Element: part of a block → .card__title
• Modifier: variation → .card--featured

Example:
.search-form { }           /* Block */
.search-form__input { }     /* Element */
.search-form__button { }    /* Element */
.search-form--dark { }      /* Modifier */

Benefits: Flat specificity (all classes), self-documenting, avoids cascading conflicts. Widely used in large codebases.

Pseudo-classes (:) select elements in a specific STATE:
• :hover, :focus, :active — interaction states
• :first-child, :nth-child(2n) — structural
• :not(.hidden), :has(.icon) — logical

Pseudo-elements (::) create virtual ELEMENTS:
• ::before, ::after — inject content
• ::placeholder — style input placeholder
• ::selection — style selected text

:has() is a game-changer:
.card:has(img) { } — style cards that contain an image
.form:has(:invalid) { } — style form when any input is invalid

cards.front: How does CSS-in-JS work and what are the tradeoffs?
cards.back:
CSS-in-JS writes styles in JavaScript (styled-components, Emotion, etc.):

const Button = styled.button`
  background: ${props => props.primary ? 'blue' : 'gray'};
  padding: 8px 16px;
`;

Pros:
• Scoped styles (no class collisions)
• Dynamic styles based on props/state
• Co-located with component code
• Dead code elimination

Cons:
• Runtime overhead (generating CSS)
• Larger bundle size
• No CSS caching separate from JS

Trend: Moving toward zero-runtime solutions (Tailwind, CSS Modules, vanilla-extract).

cards.front: What are container queries and why do they matter?
What is the cascade in CSS and how does it resolve conflicts?
What are the modern CSS units and when to use each?
cards.back:
Absolute: px (use sparingly)

Relative to font:
• em — relative to parent font-size
• rem — relative to root font-size (most predictable)
• ch — width of '0' character (great for max-width of text)

Relative to viewport:
• vw, vh — 1% of viewport width/height
• dvh — dynamic viewport height (accounts for mobile browser chrome)
• svh, lvh — small/large viewport height

Best practices:
• rem for font sizes and spacing
• % or fr for layouts
• ch for readable line lengths (max-width: 65ch)
• dvh instead of vh on mobile
